content: This appears to be an occasional bug in Thunderbird's "experimental" Account Hub feature. Try turning it off and try setting up the account again. The setting is found at <Settings | General | Account Hub> . Uncheck "Create accounts in the new Account Hub".
creator: rednoise

content:
I described above where to find the setting and how to disable it. Can you not see it?

To be more specific, it can be found at menu option (in Linux Mint) at <Edit | Settings | General | Account Hub>. If you have the menu bar hidden, you can reveal it by pressing Alt.

See the attached screenshot.

The fact that Thunderbird is running on other machines is irrelevant. You can have it going on any number of devices at any time. If everything is cranked up here at home, I could have as many as four desktop computers and a phone all checking Comcast for email at the same time.

content:
I seem to recall that Thunderbird, when it guesses at the proper settings for Comcast/Xfinity, has it in its database to use OAuth2 authentication. I find mixed messages about whether that's correct or not. I can say that Comcast/Xfinity does not mention it on their recommended email settings help page:

https://www.xfinity.com/support/articles/email-client-programs-with-xfinity-email

And I don't use OAuth2. Here are my working Comcast settings, which I use on both my Windows and Linux machines. How do they compare with yours?

Incoming Server Type: IMAP Mail Server Incoming Server Name: imap.comcast.net Port: 993 User Name: <my email address> Connection Security: SSL/TLS Authentication method: Normal Password

SMTP Server Name: smtp.comcast.net Port: 465 Connection Security: SSL/TLS Authentication method: Normal password User Name: <my email address>

I'm a little concerned that your error message mentions "localhost". Usually an email program contacts the host server directly, and localhost (meaning your local machine) isn't involved unless there is some "helper" program, like an anti-virus or other that inserts itself between Thunderbird and the network. But try the above settings and see if they work and we'll take it from there.

creator: siffemoz

content:
Try doing it this way:

Copy TB files from the old PC

- launch TB on the old PC

- go to TB menu > Help > Troubleshooting Information

- click the Application Basics > Profile Folder 'Open Folder' button

- the profile folder will open

- navigate three levels up to the AppData\Roaming folder

- close TB

- copy the AppData\Roaming\Thunderbird folder to a flash drive

---

Paste the copied TB files to the new PC

- open the AppData\Roaming folder like you did above

- delete the Thunderbird folder, or copy it somewhere if you want to save it

- replace it with the Thunderbird folder from the flash drive

- launch TB
creator: siffemoz

content:
See if this helps:

- go to TB menu > Settings > General 

- scroll all the way down and click the 'Config editor' button on the right 
- click 'Accept the risk and continue' if that appears 
- search for accessibility.force_disabled > change the value to 1 > click the check mark 
- restart TB

Also try clearing cache:

- press Ctrl-Shift-Delete (Mac: Cmd-Shift-Delete)

- set 'Time range...' to 'Everything'

- untick all items except 'Cache'

- clear, then restart TB
creator: siffemoz

To restore the Send button, do this:

  1. click the 'new message' button
  2. if menu bar is not visible, press the alt key
  3. now, click view>toolbars and tick 'composition toolbar'
creator: davidsk

content: SMTP is for 'Simple Mail Transfer Protocol' and, when initially released, was just for text. When the world decided it wanted to also send attachments, the system was adjusted to package attachments into a format that 'appeared' as text to the process, yet could be reconstructed on the receiving side. If you press Cntl and 'u' to a sent message that had an attachment, you will see an example of this. That repackaging increases the eventual size. My explanation, I'm sure, isn't 100% accurate in wording or sequence of events, but repackaging is what caused that size increase.
creator: davidsk
